narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, family background, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPA, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it estimated a 40.6% probability of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that led to denying your application, as schools aim to admit those with the strongest predicted odds of success. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to peers, signaling to the model a potential struggle in sustaining strong performance. On a more positive note, your high family income bracket worked strongly in your favor, suggesting better resources and support that often correlate with bar success. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, slightly below the typical 3.21 average, also nudged the prediction toward passing, showing solid college achievement. Similarly, your LSAT score of 29, though under the average of 36.70, still contributed positively by demonstrating some analytical strength. Your low Year 1 decile had a milder push toward failure, while planning full-time studies barely influenced the outcome, as most students do the same. Compared to typical students, your academic deciles stand out as lower than average, which weighed heavily here, even as your background advantages helped balance things. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your profile stacked up against historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening later-year grades if possible, and highlighting any additional experiences like leadership or community work that show your resilience as a Black woman in law. You have the drive; with targeted improvements, you can reapply stronger and turn this into a stepping stone. Keep pushing—you belong in this profession.
